title: Cyclopogon elatus
intro: Cyclopogon elatus, also known as 'Guinea flag' or 'Wild Iris', is a species of flowering plant in the family Iridaceae, characterized by its distinctive, long-lasting blooms and slender, grass-like foliage. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly in Brazil, to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Cyclopogon elatus include iridoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Cyclopogon elatus is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it is also used to make topical creams and ointments for wound healing and skin care.
taxonomy: Cyclopogon elatus is a species of orchid in the family Orchidaceae. The genus Cyclopogon is a group of orchids native to tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas. The family Orchidaceae is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, often referred to as the orchid family. Cyclopogon elatus is further classified within the subfamily Orchidoideae, one of the two main subfamilies of the Orchidaceae. This classification helps to group plants with similar characteristics and evolutionary histories together.
